notes.text:
A knowledge base you never revisit is a write-only archive; a daily recap fixes that.

- One message a day summarizing what was added, grouped by where it came from.
- Compute "what's new" from the wiki's git history, not post dates — otherwise a compiler catching up on a backlog makes old posts masquerade as today's news.

Raw inputs should be append-only ground truth.

- Verbatim copies of what was said, deterministic filenames, never edited, never deleted.
- Re-running an importer must be a no-op for anything already on disk.
- "Provenance dies the first time someone 'cleans up' the raw layer."

The wiki needs two layers, kept strictly apart.

- Sources layer: one faithful note per saved post — what the author actually said, attributed, no editorializing.
- Concepts layer: synthesized articles across sources; every claim links back to a source note.
- "Mixing the layers is how knowledge bases rot. The moment a 'concept' contains a claim nobody actually made, you can't trust any of it."
- A linter can enforce the split mechanically: every concept claim cites a source note, every link resolves, every source carries author, url, and date.

A file-first knowledge base beats a chat-history one.

- Bookmark a post into a folder; a pipeline turns it into a wiki page you can grep.
- "The unit of curation is the bookmark — if it wasn't worth saving, it isn't knowledge yet."
